titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 19 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Lieutenant-Colonel Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.
In this letter Spender is writing to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1916 served at Advanced General Headquarters, his duties mainly involving the writing of reports for the cabinet, for the Press, and for posterity. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.
In this letter, Spender discusses Lillian's imminent move to a new house, and the problem of what is to be done with her maids, Maggie and Sarah. He alludes to his and Lillian's pet-names for each other, 'Pink Rabbit' and 'Brown Wolf'. He recounts a recent trip to a Division which has achieved great success in the fighting, and confesses that this experience at the front has proven that much of the report he is writing is 'out'. Spender briefly discusses his toothache, his diet, the wet weather, and a budget he has attached. Spender emphasises his pride in the work Lillian is doing with the Comforts Fund, stating that 'it probably means more to the men and to the Division than we ever imagine'. He finishes with an anecdote about a much-edited communique.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
1 Aug. 19. D1633/1/1/391 You are evidently needing a holiday pretty badly
old thing & I am much concerned. It seems doubtful
if you ought to put it off till you move and I'm
afraid the bother of moving & house hunting will
also upset the pr tho she could of course use
24 as a temporary home. If it were not for Mater's
deafness it really might have done for you with the
study as your room, but I expect your are better
alone after your experiment. As to the maids
cannot you make a special case & keep them on
after the month especially as the cause of
the bother will then have ended — the 2 masters —
Perhaps I could be brought in as a special reason for
any apparent weakness. I really feel inclined to plead
for them as I believe they are fond of you and would
be prepared to go to the country later. However you
will be able to settle this better yourself tho ' I hate
one lapse causing a complete estrangement especially
in this hot weather. If you decide this couldn't
Sarah be sent on a holiday for 10 days. I believe
Maggie could do all the work & perhaps be the better
for it - tho' it sounds like rewarding the naughty— I really am concerned old thing and it is no good
going on with your work just too long, & isn't
fair on your work either in the end. You end your letter the pr's bw! At
other times I should treat this as part playfulness
to be treated with surprised & dignified growls, but
I rather wonder if it is not a tired little pr. If she
is winning the b w's sympathy under false pretences
in this there will be some distinct pink bw paw
marks later! I do not know if Bagley wood
with one of the sisters in town or the seaside
would be best but expect you will not at first
2 D1633/1/1/391 Aug. 19 feel fit enough for children and romps
which you would get at Crowborough or
Rugby tho' this might be all right later. I had a pleasant outing yesterday to
the — the Division which has done very
well in the war. The GSO is an
old SC friend and I went to get local
colour, but got something more for I
found that much that I have written
is hopelessly out & that this ice is
very thin indeed - much too thin for
a b w to walk over without a pr in
front as a guide. I think I am bound
to go in & to find the water under the
ice distinctly hot (!) but it won't be the
first time the bw has been in hot
water. He never was good at skating. Toothache quite gone tho' rather
unexpectedly & I slept well last night
tho' very late indeed ! Perhaps it suits
me after my big dinner: I have
cut down out my butter at dinner &
think I shall have to cut one course
too if I don't find I keep fit. Poor
greedy bw. A letter from C really rather
a nuisance as I know I answered his,
but I suppose as it is kindness of heart
I ought to be glad. Wet weather today when we
especially did not want it here, & I
did not want it for you as I hoped
you would get a beautiful garden
large. Perhaps you may tomorrow. This is a fairly thick budget
for you tho' I cannot pretend there
is much in it except a good deal of
love for the bw's pr!
from the bw
WB Spender
P.T.O
If I don't enthuse about your
work you know that I am very proud of
what my pr is doing. I think it probably
means more to the men and to the Division
that even we imagine, and I am quite
sure Sredward and the Ulster people & the
cause will owe a lot to you for prevent—
ing the divisions and soreness that would
be bound to come if the thing was
mismanaged, or not done at all. I hear the Daily Mail
of Wed Thursday or Friday
or Saturday last week
backed up the Division
Did you see it I had an amusing experience
today —After writing my communication
I showed it to Gen C who said it is too
cold in tone & added a flamboyant first
para & made some amendments as
I also did. I missed Gen' B, so
sent it up to the Chateau where
the CGS got it & proceeded to demolish
it. He sent for me & he complained
that it was much too Daily Maily
